sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE]
Uh huh.
In the army, which I was in charge in this particular time, I was [NON-ENGLISH] from this particular part.
I got 45 people under me.
I got several people which was anti-Semitism.
And he was from the, from the, the people, which I-- I knew everybody.
I got all the writing from who he comes, who he-- he was from the Arabs descent.
One guy, his name was-- a terrible guy-- was Shchepetkin, the name.
I will never forget.
He want to kill me twice.
But he didn't.
He forgot.
Yeah.
Because I hit him before.
Yeah.
In the school.
And I was-- I was learning.
And I was mostly, I think, smart as a, as a lot of different people.
And after that, I was going to school.
I study all the time.
And the other people didn't study.
They slept.
And I study.
That's right, to a certain way.
Also, I, I learn-- I learn a lot of Judo and karate, and I-- self defense.
I'm an expert.
Maybe today, I am already over 36 years old.
Then I'm-- why are you laughing?
Then I'm a little over.
Then I, I, I'm still OK.
This I can tell you.
I'm still OK.
I'm good with knife throwing.
Very good.
I learned the skills also part in the army.
Yeah, part in the army, yeah.
And I learned by big people, very big-- famous.
Famous, famous, famous Judo artists, you know, all kind.
I-- we, we-- they bring us-- they bring in these people from Moscow.
I have it written down.
I don't remember.
Yeah, that's right, maybe here, too, also, yeah.
And we learn a lot of tricks, very interesting tricks.
I will explain you again one more thing, why I'm rosing fast.
I didn't say to anybody.
But I will tell you again.
To rose in the Russian army, to be somebody, you must join the Communist party.
I didn't.
I joined-- I was young.
I would join the Communist young party, [NON-ENGLISH] they call them.
Until you joined, you go through the interview, you-- not like you interview.
But they interview you 10 times every week, till they let you in to [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was in [NON-ENGLISH].
And I give you my number, if you want to.
I never give to anybody.
The number in Russian is [RUSSIAN.] I will explain you in another, another language.
And-- and this-- when I received-- this is a number only.
But when I received the book, this is the number which I got.
A book-- I received a book, the [NON-ENGLISH], of the [NON-ENGLISH] party.
And this, I got open doors, complete open doors.
I came to meetings with big company, with big officers.
And this, this, this only, in the front, in the army, you can rise.
If you're not a commie, you couldn't rise.
Impossible, you couldn't do it.
They did not let you into school.
Oh, yeah.
I'm not a communist.
I'm telling you right away, OK?
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
That's right.
I knew.
I did.
Yeah.
And this bring me up.
And I, I, I was very big in the future.
I was, I was not a small man in the future.
Before, I was a very small man.
[LAUGHS] But after that, I was very big.
And they got respect for me.
OK.
No.
I knew.
I knew that this [INAUDIBLE]--
Anti-Semitism in Russia, because I feeled it.
But to know about the Novosibirsk or Novosibirsk [NON-ENGLISH], or this, I, I knew it is.
But I didn't knew the, the people which was something like that.
I knew it is, yeah.
No.
[INAUDIBLE]
We know about Auschwitz.
We know about Birkenau.
We know about Dachau.
We know.
Yeah.
Mm-hm, mm-hm.
I became an officer in '43.
[NON-ENGLISH] is not officer.
[NON-ENGLISH] is before officer.
Yeah.
Fighting.
Yeah, not a soldier.
I was already higher.
I was already a kapo.
I was already higher.
I had two stripes already.
Before [NON-ENGLISH] is two stripes.
It's not-- for me it's not interested.
The battle of Berlin was more interested.
And the, and the finish the war was more interested than the Battle of Stalingrad.
It was in the paper.
It was very big, the Battle of Stalingrad, you see.
But for professionals, it was not, not big.
We-- we lose too many people by Stalingrad, for in the, the-- which a lot of time at meetings, I criticize.
I mean, I stand up.
And I criticize.
They bring in people from different part of the world.
They threw them in like, like, like cattle.
And it was no good.
I criticize a lot.
I only criticize in the time when I knew I will be a [NON-ENGLISH] do it.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Every-- they ask me, what my family was doing, what things?
I didn't tell them the truth.
I tell them I made up my mind what I told them.
And this was the way I told them.
Just, I hold it till the last minute.
I didn't change anything.
I-- they asked me what my father was doing.
I said my father was a working man, no store, nothing.
No, no.
Religion didn't-- and if, if you apply for [NON-ENGLISH], they didn't ask any religion, because you wouldn't be-- you're not religious.
If you, if you tried to get in to this particular part, they didn't ask this question.
Some officers, there was also anti-Semitism.
But they respect me, because I, I was a top-- I mean, even if I was small, I mean, in, in rank, I was a top man.
I never was lonely.
I didn't see him.
When we, when we part, I didn't see him at all later on.
I was looking for him in Israel, because they told me he is-- I couldn't find him.
I got friends, officers friends, yeah.
I got officers also, a couple officers Jewish people.
Yeah.
I got-- I got.
I'm sorry.
I interrupt you.
I got a, a medal, won a, a big medal, I got because officer [NON-ENGLISH] Kuznetsov, he was Jewish.
And he, he was going in a place which he don't need to, to go.
And it was all mines.
And he got in in this particular place-- it was, it was a special mark that he shouldn't go.
I mean, nobody should go.
He did.
I don't know.
Maybe he was drunk.
I don't know.
And, and he blow up.
And I was the person-- I was not far from there.
A lot of people was afraid to go in.
He was still alive.
I got in.
[LAUGHS] I'm, I'm an expert, how.
I, I, I was in-- and, and I was in, not in going like that.
I was moving.
And I, I checked.
And I took, I took him out.
I move in-- you see, when you put mines, you see a point on the mines all the time, because if somebody want take off the mines, he know where he put the mine.
And I was going between the mines.
I, I was crawling, yeah.
I was crawling.
Not only crawling, I was-- it took me-- it took me a long time till I got to him.
And it took me a long time till I got him out.
I got him out.
And I received a big [RUSSIAN], a, a decoration.
[RUSSIAN], one of the biggest [RUSSIAN], before [RUSSIAN].
I'm sorry.
[RUSSIAN].
It's a very big [RUSSIAN].
You don't have too many generals get this [RUSSIAN].
I was already a [NON-ENGLISH].
OK.
That's OK.
Major Kuznetsov, a major, a major.
He was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was a big, big officer.
Sorry.
Was not in a battle at all, because we was on the front.
And we mined-- we put mines-- I didn't put it.
But we have special people which belong to the mine group.
And they put mines that the Germans, when they want to come over on this side, they blow up.
That's all what we do.
This the mine people especially.
Oh, yeah, not hear.
You see, we-- between-- the distance between the German line and, and the Russian line is different distances.
Some distance is 10 yards.
And some distance is 25 yards.
And, and in the line, in the line, the, the real soldiers, the regular soldiers, stay in this line.
And in the line is special bunkers on the side, which the soldiers, if he is not on patrol, he sleeps there.
And our people are behind the line.
Let me see, like we and [NON-ENGLISH] is, [NON-ENGLISH] is reconnaissance groups.
We stay normally with the [NON-ENGLISH].
For [NON-ENGLISH] means-- [NON-ENGLISH] is a, a big office from the commander from the [NON-ENGLISH].
From the-- a [NON-ENGLISH] is a, is a-- higher the battalion.
Each [NON-ENGLISH] has three battalions.
And each division has three [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is-- in English, I forgot already.
But I will remind you-- I'll remind so I give you.
This is headquarters from the, from the-- from this particular group.
And we are behind, we behind the front line normally a mile.
But what we do, my people, the reconnaissance people, walking mostly in the first lines, not all of them.
Whenever we have work to do, we make plans.
We sketch up which ways to go behind the German lines, and how to catch the Germans.
We calling it-- we catching is [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] in Russian is a tongue.
[NON-ENGLISH] is a tongue.
In Russian, we say, we will catch [NON-ENGLISH].
We will pick.
We go down there.

And we catch somebody, because we need that the person be interviewed from me or from somebody else, and where the stop is and where this is and how many people he have and when they came, everything.
And this we-- this is our operation.
This is [NON-ENGLISH].
We are organizing the catching the Germans.
I am an expert.
I can, I can, I can, I can pick, pick her up in five minutes.
I will tell you.
I will tell you.
We, we make plans.
And then we have special-- special [INAUDIBLE], special-- not light up, but big ones.
And we looking-- big one, very big one.
Field glasses.
And we look.
And we see all the movements.
And what kind of people staying down there, when they change the hours, which they go in, and another one comes in.
We check the mines, where the mines are.
Or there's no mines.
We-- before we do our, our job, before we do something, we know-- not 100%, because a lot of the time I lost a lot of people, too.
But we know about 90%.
We know what we're doing.
And when we catch, we don't, we don't-- when we go in to the German side already [INAUDIBLE], is a group of people which goes in.
The group of people are professionals.
It's four people, four people.
They are professional in Judo, in karate, in knife throwing, in clubs, everything.
And they have only a gun.
They don't have machine guns.
We have machine guns, all of them around.
And then this four people doing the job-- now this is the four they're calling it [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is the people which catching people.
That's all what they're doing, nothing else.
[NON-ENGLISH] is people which is cut out, a left cut and a right cut.
The left cut and right cut is normally between five and six people each side, which they do nothing.
Only they protect this four people.
They don't let in anybody.
If something happened, they fight him away.
After that, we have [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is a group which is behind.
And we cover up all of them.
And this is-- whenever I go for a big job-- I did, I don't go anymore.
But whenever I did go to a big job, I made a sketch how to do it and which way to do it.
And I'm the person which is in charge, nobody else.
I don't care it's a general.
He must listen to me.
Or he's a major.
It's not important.
I am the top man.
And this is the way I make the [INAUDIBLE].
And normally, we have a, a group, 140 people.
[NON-ENGLISH] they're calling it.
Everything is automats.
And the protectors-- then I, I told the, the commander.
He is a major.
And I tell him I need 28 people or 50 people or 40 people.
It's different how far we doing.
We doing small, I need less people.
And they are behind us to cover us if something happen.
And we, we did organize very good.
Yeah, I was one of the top professionals.
And when it was meetings from our group in the [NON-ENGLISH] division or [NON-ENGLISH] army, I was speaking.
I was the top man.
Yeah, I-- yeah, all the time.
98%, oh, yeah.
I, I, I run this place.
I, I didn't tell him to do it.
I tell him to do it.
But I run the place.
I was with them [INAUDIBLE].
Frightened, frightened, I, I, I was never frightened.
Why should I be frightened?
I was never frightened.
We have got-- we have got grenades with us.
I got four all the time.
Something happen, I blow myself up.
That's over.
It's no use to, to, to be and do it.
That's all.
Yeah.
I talk to him immediately.
I mean, when I took him out, I talked to him immediately.
I told him right away, I'm Jewish.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, all the questions.
We ask them, normally-- first of all, we start from what city he is, his name, his birth, whatever it is.
After that, what his family doing, same thing as you ask.
And done it slowly.
You know, we don't move in right away.
And they knew, if, if they don't take-- if they don't tell me the truth, I know more or less what division is in the front.
I mean, I, I, I knew when they, when they changed the division.
Also, we knew-- we was working on it, to catch the people from the new one.
And we, we knew a lot of things.
We, we write up everything, what everybody say.
And we transfer him to the higher division, to [NON-ENGLISH] division.
And one problem I got one time-- and I, I got a big [NON-ENGLISH] on it.
I catch the General Felkers from 27 [NON-ENGLISH].
I did a big job.
And I made it.
And when I carry him, I, I drink a little bit too much vodka.
And, and I'm not joking.
And, and I was on a horse.
You know, he was going.
And I was on the horse.
I took off his, his boots.
And I put it on, on myself.
He has got tremendous boots.
And it fit in my, my, my things.
And when I came to the [NON-ENGLISH] division, I gave him over.
And he was complaining, I must give him, I must give him back the boots.
General Felkers, from 27 [NON-ENGLISH].
He was, he was a big shot.
He was, he was thinking I kill him.
I said, I wouldn't kill you.
He's still alive, I think.
Yeah, I think-- I think so.
He was-- maybe he is not alive.
I don't know.
In this time, he was-- he was in the 30s, something.
Only reconnaissance.
I don't-- I don't-- I, I fight.
If somebody hit me, I fight him back.
But I-- only reconnaissance.
That's all.
I was an expert in reconnaissance and always that, that I did, nothing else.
We didn't mention.
The only person which I hit is when I took away-- when he was from the 16 Panzer Division, SS.
And he was in Auschwitz.
And he told me.
And I find these, these, these pictures.
I find, I find these pictures, the whole things.
This is the way I took it out from him.
And-- and I don't want to say what I did with him, but--
Nothing, I don't want to say nothing.
I did, yeah.
I fight plenty.
And, and I can tell you one more thing.
The German armies, which they was-- knew me, I mean the name.
I-- a lot of time, I came out to the front.
And I through a horn, I tell them I'm Jewish.
And my name is Alex Reifer.
I told them everything.
No.
I don't remember.
I have it.
But I don't remember.
I don't remember his name.
Yeah.
He was in Auschwitz.
He was an SS in Auschwitz.
For this, for this, he transfer-- they transfer him.
I don't know.
He did something wrong.
I don't know what.
He didn't tell me.
And they transfer him to the front in the SS Panzer Division.
Yeah.
He must do something wrong, because if not, the big SS people did not transfer so fast.
I killed him.
What should I tell you?
That's all that I did.
I couldn't stand it any more.
Oh, with a bullet [INAUDIBLE].
I kill many of them.
He was not the only one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
Yeah, this is the way it was.
No, nothing at all.
Maybe he killed my family.
I don't know.
I got one is nothing.
I was supposed to get more.
Yeah.
Fighting soldiers go first, always.
When we move, you mean?
You're talking about moving to a front?
Normally, when we release, release somebody else, a different division is in front.
And a different division comes down.
Then in the meantime, this division which transferred, the other division, must go in before.
And the other one leave later.
I mean, they changing.
Each part-- each, each battalion-- each battalion have several, several groups.
And each group is a special design what to do, how to do it, how to-- which part of the, of the front to take over.
Each battalion has a certain part.
Everything is, is marked down.
You couldn't jump in and take it.
Everything is marked down exactly before we move.
It's a small photo album.
This is, this is pictures.
He didn't say he took it.
But I think he took it, because if not I would kill him twice.
I only killed him one time.
And-- [LAUGHS] and this is pictures from Auschwitz, from Treblinka, from people which-- nobody can take pictures down there.
They don't take in a person like he, this gentleman which is taking now the pictures, to take pictures down there from dead people.
This is a person which was working inside.
